summary: Jeff Atwood argues that virtual machines will become the primary method of software distribution and execution. He uses VMs extensively himself to test new software in isolated environments before committing to installation. The post outlines five key advantages: security through disposable sandboxes, simplified installation via pre-configured images, OS-agnosticism, hardware acceleration from new CPU features, and the need to utilize increasingly powerful desktop hardware. Atwood predicts that VMs will eventually become first-class citizens in operating systems, creating a 'solipsist OS' where each application believes it's the only one running.
key_points: VMs provide ultimate security by allowing users to blow away potentially compromised sessions and start fresh
Software distribution as VM images eliminates complex installation processes entirely
OS choice becomes irrelevant when any application can run in its own pre-configured VM environment
Dedicated hardware virtualization from Intel and AMD will dramatically improve VM performance
Multi-core processors, terabyte drives, and expanded RAM finally provide enough power to make pervasive desktop virtualization practical
money_quotes: I don't want to junk up my primary install until I'm totally confident I know what that software does. It's guilty until proven innocent.
I think all software will eventually be distributed as virtual machine images.
No spyware or virus is virulent enough to escape a VM.
It's like formatting your hard drive every time you turn off your PC.
Who cares if your app requires Linux or OS X to run if I can boot it in a pre-configured VM within a few seconds?
Eventually, all applications will believe they're the only applications in the world. And they'll be right.
key_insight: The convergence of cheap virtualization software, hardware acceleration, and abundant computing resources will transform software distribution from complex installations to simple, isolated, disposable VM images.
